batches.name: Spec Factory - Core CLI Tools
batches.rationale: All 5 CLI tools are independent and can be built in parallel. Each tool has its own directory, CLI entry point, and business logic. Shared utilities are minimal (LiteLLM client, file I/O) and can be duplicated or created as simple modules.

tasks.title: Build Spec Generator CLI - AI-powered spec creation from one-liners
tasks.prompt:
Build the Spec Generator CLI tool that transforms rough app concepts into detailed Jules-ready specs.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory for accelerating micro-saas ecosystem development
- Full spec at: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md
- Tech stack: Node.js, oclif CLI framework, Claude Sonnet 4.6 via LiteLLM
- LiteLLM already running at http://localhost:4000 in Docker stack
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. CLI command: npm run spec-gen -- <appName> <cluster> <one-liner>
2. Reads template patterns from tools/spec-generator/templates/:
   - cluster-{{A-K}}-template.md (cluster-specific patterns)
   - common-sections.json (required sections: Problem, Solution, Data Model, Integration Manifest, Tech Stack, MVP Scope)
   - cc-starter-modules.json (available modules and when to use them)
3. Generates spec using AI (Claude Sonnet 4.6 via LiteLLM API):
   - POST to http://localhost:4000/models/claude-sonnet-4.6/generate
   - Prompt: "Generate detailed spec for {appName} in {cluster}. Context: {oneLiner}. Follow structure from template. Ensure integration with Nexus Hub and cc-starter."
4. Validates structure: checks all required sections present, validates JSON schemas
5. Saves spec to docs/superpowers/specs/{cluster}/{appName}-spec.md:
   - Markdown with YAML frontmatter (cluster, appName, generatedAt, aiModel, confidence)
   - Human-readable format
6. Outputs confidence score (0-1) based on AI response quality

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tools/spec-generator/
├── cli.js                 # oclif command entry point
├── generator.js           # main generation logic
├── llm-client.js          # LiteLLM API client
├── templates/             # cluster-specific templates
│   ├── cluster-a-template.md
│   ├── cluster-b-template.md
│   └── ...
├── common-sections.json   # required sections schema
└── cc-starter-modules.json # available modules

INTEGRATION:
- Uses LiteLLM at http://localhost:4000 (already in Docker stack)
- Reads existing spec patterns from docs/superpowers/specs/cluster-a-detailed-specs.md
- Creates specs in same format as existing cluster-a specs

ERROR HANDLING:
- Handle LiteLLM API failures (retry 3x with exponential backoff)
- Validate appName is alphanumeric with hyphens
- Validate cluster is A-K
- Create directory if doesn't exist

TESTING:
- Create 2-3 example specs to verify it works
- Test with: npm run spec-gen -- TestApp cluster-a "Test app for validation"

Make it production-ready with proper error handling, logging, and CLI help text.

tasks.title: Build Spec Validator CLI - AI-powered quality checks and auto-fix
tasks.prompt:
Build the Spec Validator CLI tool that performs quality checks on generated specs before dispatching to Jules.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md)
- Tech stack: Node.js, oclif CLI framework, Claude Sonnet 4.6 via LiteLLM
- Validates specs created by Spec Generator
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. CLI command: npm run spec-validate -- <specPath>
2. Parses spec: reads markdown + YAML frontmatter, extracts all sections
3. Runs parallel validation checks:
   **Structural checks** (regex/schema validation):
   - All required sections present
   - Integration manifest valid JSON with correct schema
   - Data model entities follow cc-starter naming conventions
   - Port numbers don't conflict (check infra/ports-registry.json if exists, else use 8091-8099 range)
   
   **Semantic checks** (AI-powered via Claude):
   - Problem statement is clear and specific
   - Solution actually addresses stated problem
   - MVP scope is realistic for 4-6 hour Jules build
   - Integration events follow naming conventions (camelCase, past tense)
   - Data model doesn't duplicate existing apps (scan docs/superpowers/specs/)
   
   **Cross-app checks**:
   - No duplicate app names across all clusters
   - Integration events don't conflict (same name, different payload)
   - Port allocations unique

4. Outputs validation report (JSON + console):
   - valid: true/false
   - confidence: 0-1
   - checks: { structural, problem_clarity, solution_alignment, mvp_scope, integration_compatibility, port_availability }
   - warnings: []
   - suggestions: []

5. Auto-fix capabilities (optional --fix flag):
   - Reformat integration manifest to match schema
   - Assign available port if not specified
   - Standardize event naming
   - Add missing sections with AI-generated content

QUALITY GATES:
- confidence < 0.7 → Requires human review (warn but don't block)
- structural = fail → Block execution, show errors
- warnings present → Ask for confirmation before continuing

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tools/spec-validator/
├── cli.js              # oclif command
├── validator.js        # main validation logic
├── checks/
│   ├── structural.js   # regex/schema checks
│   ├── semantic.js     # AI-powered semantic checks
│   └── cross-app.js    # cross-app conflict detection
└── schemas/
    ├── integration-manifest.json
    └── data-model.json

INTEGRATION:
- Uses LiteLLM at http://localhost:4000 for semantic validation
- Reads all specs from docs/superpowers/specs/ for cross-app checks
- Outputs to console and .validation-reports/<appName>-report.json

Make it robust with comprehensive checks and clear error messages.

tasks.title: Build Enhanced Jules Orchestrator - Intelligent queuing and PR management
tasks.prompt:
Build the Enhanced Jules Orchestrator that handles the 5-session limit with intelligent queuing, retry logic, and automatic PR merging.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md)
- Enhances existing scripts/dispatch-cluster-a.sh and scripts/monitor-jules-cluster-a.sh
- Tech stack: Node.js, oclif CLI framework, Jules CLI integration
- Must respect Jules limits: 5 concurrent sessions, 60 tasks/day
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. CLI command: npm run jules-batch -- <cluster> <concurrency>
2. Discovers specs: finds all un-built apps in cluster (check docs/superpowers/specs/<cluster>/*.md vs apps/ directory)
3. Creates build queue prioritized by dependencies (uses depends_on field from integration manifests)
4. Dispatches in waves (max 5 concurrent):
   ```javascript
   while (queue.hasApps()) {
     const wave = queue.take(5);
     const sessions = await dispatchToJules(wave);
     await waitForCompletion(sessions);
     validateAndMerge(sessionResults);
     queue.markCompleted(wave);
   }
   ```

5. Smart session management:
   - Polling: checks status every 5 minutes via 'jules remote list --session'
   - Timeout: kills sessions stuck in PLANNING > 30 minutes
   - Auto-retry: re-queues FAILED sessions with exponential backoff (5min, 15min, 30min)
   - State persistence: saves progress to .jules-orchestrator-state.json (survives restart)

6. PR validation & merging:
   - Pre-merge checks: runs tests, checks cc-starter integration, validates Nexus Hub registration
   - Configurable merge strategies:
     * auto_merge_passed: merge if all tests pass (default)
     * manual_review: create PR but wait for human
     * auto_merge_all: trust Jules, merge immediately
   - Rollback: if merge breaks tests, automatically revert and log to .jules-failures/

7. Progress dashboard:
   ```
   🚀 Jules Batch: cluster-b (12 apps, 3 waves)
   Wave 1: ✅✅✅✅✅ (5/5 complete)
   Wave 2: 🟡🟡🟡⏸️❌ (2/5 complete, 1 awaiting input, 1 failed)
   Wave 3: ⏸️⏸️⏸️⏸️⏸️ (queued)
   Estimated completion: 4h 23min
   ```

INTEGRATION:
- Reuses .jules-plan-*.json format (already exists in repo)
- Calls tools/scaffold-app.sh for setup
- Calls tools/register-app.sh after successful builds
- Uses Jules CLI: 'jules remote new --repo "Shashank2577/micro-saas" "description"'
- Parses session IDs from Jules output: grep "ID:" | awk '{print $2}'

ERROR RECOVERY:
- Sessions in AWAITING_USER_INPUT: send notification (console + webhook if configured)
- Sessions FAILED: save error log, retry with modified prompt
- Network issues: exponential backoff, resume from checkpoint

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tools/jules-orchestrator/
├── cli.js                # oclif command
├── orchestrator.js       # main orchestration logic
├── queue.js              # priority queue with dependency resolution
├── session-manager.js    # Jules session lifecycle management
├── pr-handler.js         # PR validation and merging
└── .jules-orchestrator-state.json  # persisted state

Build it robustly with comprehensive error handling and recovery logic.

tasks.title: Build Test Runner CLI - Automated post-build validation
tasks.prompt:
Build the Test Runner CLI that performs automated validation of Jules-built apps to ensure they actually work.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md)
- Tech stack: Node.js, oclif CLI framework, axios for HTTP calls
- Tests Spring Boot apps built by Jules using cc-starter framework
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. CLI command: npm run app-test -- <appName>
2. Spins up app in isolated environment:
   - Check Docker containers running (docker-compose ps)
   - Build app: cd apps/<appName>/backend && mvn clean package -DskipTests
   - Start app: mvn spring-boot:run
   - Wait for health check: curl http://localhost:8091/actuator/health (retry 10x with 5s delay)

3. Runs parallel test suites:
   **Smoke tests** (HTTP calls):
   - GET /actuator/health → expect 200
   - GET /v3/api-docs → expect 200 with OpenAPI JSON
   - GET /api/v1/manifest → expect { emits: [], consumes: [], capabilities: [] }
   
   **Integration tests**:
   - Register app with Nexus Hub: POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/apps/register with manifest
   - Emit test event: POST /api/v1/events { sourceApp: 'test-runner', eventType: 'test', payload: {} }
   - Call capability endpoint: POST /api/v1/capabilities/<capability-name>
   
   **cc-starter compliance tests**:
   - Tenancy: send X-Tenant-ID header, expect it to be respected
   - Auth: request without token → expect 401
   - Audit logs: check that actions create audit entries
   - Webhooks: verify /api/v1/webhooks endpoint exists

4. Collects test results:
   ```json
   {
     "appName": "incidentbrain",
     "status": "pass",
     "duration": "2m 34s",
     "tests": {
       "smoke": "pass (5/5)",
       "integration": "pass (3/3)",
       "cc-starter": "pass (4/4)"
     },
     "coverage": { "api": "85%", "workflows": "60%" },
     "issues": [],
     "metrics": {
       "startup_time": "12s",
       "memory_mb": 512,
       "db_connections": 4
     }
   }
   ```

5. Generates test report:
   - Saves to test-reports/<appName>-test-report.json
   - Creates summary badge for README
   - On failure: saves logs to test-reports/<appName>-logs/

BATCH MODE:
npm run app-test -- --batch cluster-a
# Tests all 5 apps in sequence, shows aggregate report

INTEGRATION WITH CI/CD:
- Runs automatically after Jules PR merge
- Blocks merge if tests fail (configurable via --continue-on-fail)
- Posts results to PR comments using gh CLI

PERFORMANCE BASELINES:
- Tracks app startup time, memory usage over time
- Flags regressions (>20% slower than previous build)
- Saves metrics to test-metrics/<appName>.jsonl

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tools/app-tester/
├── cli.js                  # oclif command
├── tester.js               # main testing logic
├── suites/
│   ├── smoke-tests.js      # basic HTTP tests
│   ├── integration-tests.js # Nexus Hub integration
│   └── cc-starter-tests.js # framework compliance
└── reporters/
    ├── json-reporter.js    # JSON output
    └── console-reporter.js # CLI output

Make it comprehensive with good error messages and detailed logging.

tasks.title: Create shared infrastructure and utilities for all CLI tools
tasks.prompt:
Create the shared infrastructure and common utilities that all Spec Factory CLI tools will use.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md)
- All 5 CLI tools need common utilities: LiteLLM client, file I/O, JSON schemas, CLI helpers
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. Update package.json with CLI scripts:
   ```json
   {
     "scripts": {
       "spec-gen": "node tools/spec-generator/cli.js",
       "spec-validate": "node tools/spec-validator/cli.js",
       "jules-batch": "node tools/jules-orchestrator/cli.js",
       "app-test": "node tools/app-tester/cli.js",
       "pipeline": "node tools/pipeline/cli.js"
     }
   }
   ```

2. Install dependencies:
   ```bash
   npm install --save-dev oclif@^4.0.0 axios@^1.6.0 chalk@^5.3.0 ora@^8.0.0 inquirer@^9.2.0
   npm install --save-dev jest@^29.7.0
   ```

3. Create shared utilities in tools/shared/:
   - llm-client.js: LiteLLM API client for Claude Sonnet 4.6
     * function generate(prompt, options) → returns AI response
     * Handles retries, timeout, error handling
     * Uses http://localhost:4000/models/claude-sonnet-4.6/generate
   
   - file-utils.js: common file operations
     * function readSpec(path) → parses markdown + frontmatter
     * function writeSpec(path, content) → writes with proper formatting
     * function ensureDir(path) → creates directory if needed
   
   - json-utils.js: JSON schema validation
     * function validateSchema(json, schema) → validates and returns errors
     * function loadSchema(name) → loads schema from schemas/
   
   - cli-helpers.js: common CLI patterns
     * function confirm(message) → prompts user yes/no
     * function select(message, choices) → prompts user to pick option
     * function spinner(text, fn) → shows spinner during async operation

4. Create JSON schemas in tools/shared/schemas/:
   - integration-manifest.json: schema for app integration manifests
     ```json
     {
       "type": "object",
       "required": ["emits", "consumes", "capabilities"],
       "properties": {
         "emits": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
         "consumes": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
         "capabilities": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "object"}}
       }
     }
     ```
   
   - data-model.json: schema for data model definitions
   - app-manifest.json: schema for app registration manifest

5. Create cluster templates in tools/spec-generator/templates/:
   - cluster-a-template.md: Developer Intelligence apps pattern
   - cluster-b-template.md: Observability & Monitoring apps pattern
   - cluster-c-template.md: Security & Compliance apps pattern
   - Continue for clusters D-K based on ecosystem design
   
   Each template should include:
   - Common sections: Tagline, Problem, Solution, Core Features
   - Integration Manifest template
   - Data Model patterns
   - Tech Stack defaults (Spring Boot + cc-starter, Next.js, PostgreSQL + pgvector)
   - MVP Scope guidance

6. Create tools/shared/cc-starter-modules.json:
   Catalog of all cc-starter modules and when to use them:
   ```json
   {
     "modules": {
       "tenancy": {"description": "Multi-tenant support", "use": "all apps"},
       "auth": {"description": "Keycloak authentication", "use": "apps with user login"},
       "rbac": {"description": "Role-based access control", "use": "apps with permissions"},
       "audit": {"description": "Audit logging", "use": "all apps"},
       "search": {"description": "pgvector search", "use": "apps with search/vector search"},
       "ai": {"description": "LiteLLM integration", "use": "apps with AI features"},
       "webhooks": {"description": "Webhook system", "use": "apps that emit events"},
       "notifications": {"description": "Notification system", "use": "apps that send alerts"}
     }
   }
   ```

7. Create basic unit tests for shared utilities:
   - tests/shared/llm-client.test.js
   - tests/shared/file-utils.test.js
   - tests/shared/json-utils.test.js

INTEGRATION:
- Ensure LiteLLM is accessible at http://localhost:4000
- Follow oclif best practices for CLI structure
- Use chalk for colored console output
- Use ora for spinners during async operations
- Use inquirer for interactive prompts

Create clean, well-documented, reusable utilities that all 5 CLI tools will depend on.

tasks.title: Build Pipeline Orchestrator - One-command idea to tested app
tasks.prompt:
Build the Pipeline Orchestrator that glues all 4 CLI tools together into one seamless pipeline from idea to tested, registered app.

CONTEXT:
- Part of AI-Native Spec Factory (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-16-spec-factory-design.md)
- Orchestrates: Spec Generator → Spec Validator → Jules Orchestrator → Test Runner → Nexus Registration
- Tech stack: Node.js, oclif CLI framework
- Depends on the other 4 CLI tools being implemented
- Repo: Shashank2577/micro-saas on main branch

REQUIREMENTS:
1. CLI command: npm run pipeline -- <appName> <cluster> <one-liner>
2. Executes full pipeline:
   ```javascript
   1. Spec Generator    → Creates detailed spec from one-liner
   2. Spec Validator    → Checks quality, auto-fixes issues
   3. Jules Orchestrator→ Dispatches to Jules, waits for PR
   4. Test Runner       → Validates built app
   5. Nexus Register    → Registers with ecosystem
   ```

3. Output format:
   ```
   ✅ Spec generated: docs/superpowers/specs/cluster-b/logstream-spec.md
   ✅ Spec validated: confidence 0.92, no issues
   ✅ Jules session created: 982374829374892374
   ⏳ Waiting for Jules build... (typically 4-6 hours)
      → Tracking at: https://jules.google.com
   ✅ PR created: https://github.com/Shashank2577/micro-saas/pull/42
   ✅ Tests passed: smoke (5/5), integration (3/3), cc-starter (4/4)
   ✅ Registered with Nexus Hub: http://localhost:8080/apps/logstream
   ✅ LogStream is live at: http://localhost:8092
   🎉 Pipeline complete in 4h 23m
   ```

4. State persistence:
   - Saves to .pipeline-state/<appName>.json after each stage
   - Can resume from any stage if interrupted
   - Example: npm run pipeline -- LogStream cluster-b --resume-from=jules

5. Batch mode for entire clusters:
   - npm run pipeline -- --batch cluster-a --concurrency 5
   - Processes all 5 apps in parallel
   - Shows aggregate progress
   - Full ecosystem in ~6 hours

ERROR HANDLING:
- If Spec Generator fails: show error, don't continue
- If Spec Validator fails with warnings: ask user to confirm or abort
- If Jules build fails: save error log, offer to retry or skip
- If Test Runner fails: save logs, don't register with Nexus Hub
- If Nexus registration fails: show error, app is built but not registered

PROGRESS TRACKING:
- Show spinner during long operations (Jules build)
- Update console with ETA based on average times:
   * Spec gen: 30s
   * Spec validation: 20s
   * Jules build: 4-6 hours
   * Testing: 2-5 min per app
   * Registration: 10s

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tools/pipeline/
├── cli.js              # oclif command
├── orchestrator.js     # main pipeline logic
└── stages/
    ├── spec-gen.js     # calls spec-generator
    ├── validate.js     # calls spec-validator
    ├── jules.js        # calls jules-orchestrator
    ├── test.js         # calls app-tester
    └── register.js     # calls register-app.sh

INTEGRATION:
- Calls other CLI tools via child_process.exec()
- Uses tools/register-app.sh for Nexus Hub registration
- Reads .jules-orchestrator-state.json to track Jules progress
- Creates .pipeline-state/ directory for checkpoints

CONFIGURATION:
- Support --concurrency flag for batch mode (default: 5)
- Support --resume-from=<stage> flag for resuming
- Support --skip-tests flag to skip testing (not recommended)
- Support --dry-run flag to show what would happen

Make it robust with excellent error handling and progress reporting.
